content:
# Matthew — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Matthew (Greek: Kata Matthaion) | **Chapters:** 28

## At a Glance
- **Author:** The text is anonymous — no internal attribution. The title "According to Matthew" (kata Matthaion) was added by the 2nd century CE. Papias (c. 130 CE, preserved in Eusebius, Church History 3.39.16) states that "Matthew collected the oracles (logia) in the Hebrew language, and each interpreted them as best he could." Whether this refers to the canonical Gospel — which was composed in Greek, not translated from Hebrew — is debated. The author demonstrates detailed knowledge of Jewish law, scripture, and interpretive methods.
- **Date:** c. 80–90 CE is the majority scholarly estimate. The destruction of the temple (70 CE) appears to be reflected in 22:7 ("The king was enraged. He sent his army and destroyed those murderers and burned their city"). Matthew uses Mark as a source, placing it after Mark (c. 65–70 CE).
- **Audience:** A Jewish-Christian community, likely in Syria (Antioch is the most common proposal). The text assumes familiarity with Jewish customs, debates about Torah observance, and synagogue practice.
- **Genre:** Ancient biography (bios) — a narrative of Jesus' life organized thematically and theologically, not strictly chronologically.
- **Timeline:** Narrative: c. 6/4 BCE (birth) to c. 30/33 CE (death and resurrection). Composition: c. 80–90 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** Matthew presents Jesus as the Jewish Messiah who fulfills the Hebrew scriptures — structuring his teaching into five major discourses, opening with a genealogy tracing David's line, and quoting the Old Testament more than any other Gospel.

## The World Behind the Text
The Synoptic Problem is foundational for reading Matthew. Matthew, Mark, and Luke share extensive material — often in the same order and with identical Greek wording. The dominant scholarly solution (the Two-Source Hypothesis): Matthew and Luke independently used Mark as a narrative framework and a second source called "Q" (from German Quelle, "source") for shared sayings material not found in Mark. Matthew also contains material unique to it ("M" source). Alternative hypotheses exist (Farrer, Griesbach/Two-Gospel), but the Two-Source Hypothesis remains the majority position.

After 70 CE, Judaism was reconstituting itself without the temple. The Pharisaic movement was becoming dominant (leading to rabbinic Judaism). Matthew's fierce polemic against "scribes and Pharisees" (chapter 23) likely reflects competitive tension between the Matthean community and the emerging rabbinic movement — both claiming to be the authentic heirs of Israel's tradition.

The earliest manuscript evidence for Matthew includes P104 (c. 150 CE, containing Matthew 21:34–37), P77 (c. 150–200 CE), and P64+67 (c. 200 CE, the Magdalen Papyrus). Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus (4th century) contain the complete text.

## Why This Book Was Written
Matthew's organizing principle is fulfillment. The formula "this took place to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet" (or variations) appears approximately 12 times (1:22, 2:15, 2:17, 2:23, 4:14, 8:17, 12:17, 13:14, 13:35, 21:4, 26:56, 27:9). Each citation connects an event in Jesus' life to a Hebrew Bible text. The citations draw from Isaiah, Hosea, Micah, Jeremiah, Zechariah, and Psalms — the interpretive method resembles pesher exegesis known from Qumran.

The five major discourses — Sermon on the Mount (5–7), Mission Discourse (10), Parables (13), Community Discourse (18), Olivet Discourse (24–25) — each end with a formula: "When Jesus had finished saying these things" (7:28, 11:1, 13:53, 19:1, 26:1). The five-fold structure has been compared to the five books of the Torah, with Jesus presented as a new Moses teaching from a mountain (5:1).

## How to Read This Book
**Chapters 1–2:** Birth narrative (unique to Matthew: magi, flight to Egypt, Herod's massacre). **3–4:** Baptism and temptation. **5–7:** Sermon on the Mount. **8–9:** Healing cycle. **10:** Mission instructions. **11–12:** Conflict and rejection. **13:** Parables. **14–17:** Miracles, Peter's confession, transfiguration. **18:** Community life. **19–23:** Journey to Jerusalem, temple conflicts, woes against Pharisees. **24–25:** Olivet Discourse (end times). **26–28:** Passion, death, resurrection.

The Sermon on the Mount (chapters 5–7) contains the Beatitudes (5:3–12), the antitheses ("You have heard that it was said... but I say to you," 5:21–48), the Lord's Prayer (6:9–13), and the Golden Rule (7:12). Luke's parallel material (the "Sermon on the Plain," Luke 6:17–49) is shorter and differently arranged — a comparison illuminates each Gospel's editorial choices.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
Matthew's genealogy (1:1–17) traces Jesus through David to Abraham — establishing Israelite and royal lineage. The genealogy is structured in three sets of 14 generations (1:17), though the count requires some compression. Four women are named: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and "the wife of Uriah" (Bathsheba) — all with irregular or controversial circumstances in their biblical narratives.

Matthew 5:17–20 states: "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them." This verse is central to debates about the relationship between Jesus and Torah — Matthew's Jesus intensifies Torah demands rather than relaxing them.

The "Great Commission" (28:18–20) — "Go and make disciples of all nations" — is unique to Matthew and became the foundational text for Christian mission theology.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **Matthew 27:9–10 attributes a quotation to Jeremiah** that is actually closest to Zechariah 11:12–13 (thirty pieces of silver). This cross-attribution is a textual fact noted since antiquity — various explanations have been proposed but none is definitive.
- **The formula quotations** sometimes follow the Hebrew text, sometimes the Septuagint, and sometimes neither — suggesting the author worked with both traditions or used a different text form.
- **"Emmanuel" (1:23, quoting Isaiah 7:14)** uses the Septuagint's parthenos ("virgin") for the Hebrew almah ("young woman"). Matthew builds the virgin birth narrative on the Greek translation.
- **Peter's prominence in Matthew** — receiving the "keys of the kingdom" (16:19), being named the "rock" (petra/Petros, 16:18) on which the church is built — is unique to this Gospel. The Aramaic wordplay (Kepha/kepha, "rock/rock") works more naturally than the Greek.
- **The parable of the sheep and goats (25:31–46)** — judgment based on treatment of "the least of these" — is unique to Matthew and has no Synoptic parallel.
- **"Synagogue" language (4:23, 9:35, 10:17, 12:9, 13:54)** — Matthew refers to "their synagogues," implying the author's community is distinct from the synagogue. This phrasing suggests a community in the process of separating from the broader Jewish community.

## Timeline
- **c. 65–70 CE:** Gospel of Mark composed (Matthew's primary source)
- **70 CE:** Destruction of the Jerusalem temple
- **c. 80–90 CE:** Matthew composed
- **c. 130 CE:** Papias's testimony about Matthew
- **c. 150–200 CE:** Earliest papyrus fragments (P104, P77)
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus (complete text)

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
